insight: In a pure Gauss Cannon (Tarsier) mirror endgame with no other units, the winning and losing board states follow a Fibonacci-like sequence based on total health of units on each side; the player who first reaches a losing position in that sequence loses the race.
author: consensus
context: Expert players including extratricky, mrguy888, and spiritfryer collaboratively derived and verified lookup tables of winning/losing board states in Gauss Cannon mirror endgames, referencing an Elyot Reddit post on the Fibonacci pattern.

insight: In a Gauss Cannon endgame where one player also has a Blastforge, killing the forge early (when the 3rd cannon is dying, not the 4th) is the correct timing; after the forge kill the board state transitions into the standard forgeless Gauss Cannon winning/losing sequence.
author: consensus
context: mrguy888 and extratricky discussed and corrected each other on the precise turn to kill a Blastforge in a Gauss Cannon endgame, building lookup tables for forge-present winning positions.

insight: Tarsier (Gauss Cannon) mirror endgame winning/losing positions, expressed as total HP on board per side, follow a Fibonacci-like ratio; the equivalent analysis for a different unit (referred to as 'geese') follows a different sequence with a ratio of approximately 0.91 rather than 0.618, and has additional irregularities from off-by-one kills.
author: extratricky
context: extratricky explained the mathematical structure of Gauss Cannon and 'geese' endgame sequences when spiritfryer asked whether the Fibonacci pattern applied to both.

insight: liadahlia recommends that players be able to beat Master Bot consistently before transitioning to ranked PvP, treating consistent MB wins as a benchmark of readiness for competitive play.
author: liadahlia
context: Discussion about the recommended progression path from campaign/combat training to ranked PvP for new players.

insight: Wincer was buffed by one gold at some point because the community broadly thought it was too weak; additionally, early players did not know whether to click or hold Omega Splitter when walls were present, with top player DB being an Omega clicker — illustrating how much game understanding has improved over time.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 cited these examples to support the claim that high-level Prismata play has improved substantially since early access.
